sources: RFF: How Long Does It Take? (2025)

sectors.name: Solar

metrics.capacityNote: Only 10% of total U.S. solar increase 2010-2023

sectors.keyFinding: Fastest EIS completion of any energy sector: 27-month avg (NOI→ROD, RFF 2025). 32% cancellation rate (Bennon & Wilson, 2023)

sectors.name: Wind

metrics.capacityNote: Only 3.7% of total U.S. wind increase 2010-2023

sectors.keyFinding: 50% EIS litigation rate — highest of any clean energy sector (RFF, 2025). 50% meet 2-year target vs. 67% for solar

sectors.name: Transmission
sectors.keyFinding: Average 10-year end-to-end project timeline (not formal NEPA only), with ~80% of time spent waiting for approval. Lowest cancellation rate (12%)

sectors.name: Geothermal

metrics.multipleReviewsNote: A single geothermal project can require up to 6 separate NEPA reviews

sectors.keyFinding: EAs perform well (92% meet 1-year target), but single projects face up to 6 sequential NEPA reviews for different phases

sectors.name: Highways & Transportation
sectors.keyFinding: FHWA EISs take 6.2 years on average (mean NOI→ROD, 2010–2018, CEQ) — longest of any major agency
sectors.caveat: Cost increase is correlation, not proven causation

sectors.name: Public Lands & Forestry
sectors.keyFinding: 37% of all NEPA appellate litigation targets public lands (Breakthrough, 2024). Forest Service produces the most EISs (18% of total) and takes 5.1 years on average (CEQ)

sectors.name: Mining & Minerals
sectors.keyFinding: 9% of NEPA litigation. Agency win rate: 76% — below the 80% overall average (Breakthrough, 2024)

sectors.name: Water Infrastructure
sectors.keyFinding: Army Corps handles 12% of EISs. 85% agency win rate in litigation — highest of any sector
